original_abstract:
Nitrate ($NO_{3}^{-}$) contamination in water sources poses a significant threat to global health and ecological stability, necessitating the development of efficient removal technologies. While bimetallic palladium-copper (Pd/Cu) systems are known to facilitate the nitrate reduction reaction (NRR), the comparative performance of Pd single atoms versus nanoparticles across different catalytic regimes remains poorly understood. In this study, we synthesized a library of Pd/Cu catalysts ranging from Pd-rich alloys to Pd/Cu(1:100) single-atom alloys (SAAs) to evaluate their performance in thermocatalytic (T-NRR) versus electrocatalytic (E-NRR) pathways. 

Our results reveal a striking divergence in catalytic capability: while the Pd/Cu(1:100) SAA performed poorly in the thermocatalytic system, it exhibited exceptional activity ($TOF_{Pd}$ = 2 min$^{-1}$) and superior $N_{2}$ selectivity (94%) in the electrocatalytic system, outperforming its nanoalloy counterparts. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations elucidate the mechanistic origins of this contrast, attributing the enhanced E-NRR performance of the SAA to increased $NO_{3}*$ adsorption stability under alkaline electrochemical conditions. Furthermore, the localized pH environment and the ability to utilize water as a proton source in E-NRR lower the kinetic barrier for $N_{2}$ formation relative to ammonia. This work highlights the unique advantages of the SAA architecture for selective electrochemical water treatment and establishes critical design principles for transitioning between thermocatalytic and electrocatalytic processes.
humanized_abstract:
Nitrate (NO 3 −) contamination in water bodies has become a global challenge affecting public health and environmental sustainability. Exploring efficient strategies for removing nitrates from water bodies is thus urgent. As previously demonstrated, bimetallic palladium-copper (Pd/Cu) systems can catalyze the nitrate reduction reaction (NRR). Here, the catalytic activities of Pd nanoparticles (NPs) and single atoms in Pd/Cu alloys under various reaction conditions are not yet thoroughly explored. In this study, a series of Pd/Cu catalysts including Pd-rich alloys, alloys with varying Pd/Cu ratios from 1:1 to 1:100 prepared via bottom-up methods were investigated under both thermocatalytic (T-NRR) and electrocatalytic (E-NRR) conditions.

Our experiments demonstrate a striking difference in catalytic performance in different environments: while the Pd/Cu(1:100) SAA exhibited almost no activity in the thermocatalytic setup, it showed a high activity ($TOF_{Pd}$ = 2 min$^{-1}$) and high $N_{2}$ selectivity (94%) in the electrocatalytic system and even outperformed the nanoalloys. Using density functional theory (DFT) we obtain insights into the reasons for the higher E-NRR performance of the SAA in the alkaline electrolyte environment of the E-NRR experiment. Increased stability of the $NO_{3}*$ adsorbates on the SAA surface is proposed as the reason for the enhanced performance. Additionally, a lower energy barrier for $N_{2}$ formation compared to $NH_{3}$ is achieved due to the localized pH environment and the fact that in E-NRR water can be used as proton source. Our study shows the potential of the SAA concept for high-performance electrocatalytic water treatment and provides key information for the design of catalysts for successful catalysis in different environments.
